title: [DA] Introduce domain for monotonicity
author: kasuga-fj
description:
As discussed in #162281, monotonicity in DA is ill-defined, especially when a loop doesn't have an exact backedge-taken count. To address this, this patch introduces a new concept "domain" for monotonicity. A domain represents the same notion as that in the mathematical sense, indicating the set of values over which a function is monotonic. Instead of explicitly holding the set of values, for now, the domain is an enum with the following variants:

- Entire: Corresponds to the entire iteration space of the loops. This is well-defined only when all loops have exact backedge-taken counts.
- Effective: Corresponds to the set of values where the function is actually executed.

See the comments in `SCEVMonotonicityDomain` for more details.

This patch only introduces the new concept and changes the existing monotonicity queries to take a domain parameter, but doesn't change the logic of the monotonicity checker. The next patch, #176368, will update the logic to take the domain into account.

define void @sext_may_wrap(ptr %a, i8 %start, i8 %step) {
entry:
  br label %loop

loop:                                             ; preds = %loop, %entry
  %i = phi i64 [ 0, %entry ], [ %i.inc, %loop ]
  %offset = phi i8 [ %start, %entry ], [ %offset.next, %loop ]
  %offset.sext = sext i8 %offset to i64
  %idx = getelementptr i8, ptr %a, i64 %offset.sext
  store i8 0, ptr %idx, align 1
  %i.inc = add nsw i64 %i, 1
  %offset.next = add i8 %offset, %step
  %exitcond = icmp eq i64 %i.inc, 100
  br i1 %exitcond, label %exit, label %loop

exit:                                             ; preds = %loop
  ret void
}

  %gep = getelementptr inbounds i8, ptr %a, i64 %subscript
  store i8 0, ptr %gep, align 1
  br label %loop.j.latch

loop.j.latch:                                     ; preds = %if.then, %loop.j.header
  %j.inc = add nuw nsw i64 %j, 1
  %subscript.next = add nuw nsw i64 %subscript, 1
  %ec.j = icmp eq i64 %j.inc, 32
  br i1 %ec.j, label %loop.i.latch, label %loop.j.header

loop.i.latch:                                     ; preds = %loop.j.latch
  %i.inc = add nuw nsw i64 %i, 1
  %subscript.i.next = add i64 %subscript.i, 1024
  %ec.i = icmp eq i64 %i.inc, 4611686018427387904
  br i1 %ec.i, label %exit, label %loop.i.header

exit:                                             ; preds = %loop.i.latch
  ret void
}

define void @nested_loop_nsw0(ptr %a, i64 %n, i64 %m) {
entry:
  %guard.i = icmp sgt i64 %n, 0
  br i1 %guard.i, label %loop.i.header, label %exit

loop.i.header:                                    ; preds = %loop.i.latch, %entry
  %i = phi i64 [ 0, %entry ], [ %i.inc, %loop.i.latch ]
  br label %loop.j.preheader

loop.j.preheader:                                 ; preds = %loop.i.header
  %gurard.j = icmp sgt i64 %m, 0
  br i1 %gurard.j, label %loop.j, label %loop.i.latch

loop.j:                                           ; preds = %loop.j, %loop.j.preheader
  %j = phi i64 [ 0, %loop.j.preheader ], [ %j.inc, %loop.j ]
  %offset = add nsw i64 %i, %j
  %idx = getelementptr inbounds i8, ptr %a, i64 %offset
  store i8 0, ptr %idx, align 1
  %j.inc = add nsw i64 %j, 1
  %exitcond.j = icmp eq i64 %j.inc, %m
  br i1 %exitcond.j, label %loop.i.latch, label %loop.j

loop.i.latch:                                     ; preds = %loop.j, %loop.j.preheader
  %i.inc = add nsw i64 %i, 1
  %exitcond.i = icmp eq i64 %i.inc, %n
  br i1 %exitcond.i, label %exit, label %loop.i.header

exit:                                             ; preds = %loop.i.latch, %entry
  ret void
}

define void @step_is_variant2(ptr %a) {
entry:
  br label %loop.i.header

loop.i.header:                                    ; preds = %loop.i.latch, %entry
  %i = phi i64 [ 0, %entry ], [ %i.inc, %loop.i.latch ]
  %offset.i = phi i64 [ 0, %entry ], [ %offset.i.next, %loop.i.latch ]
  %step.i.0 = phi i64 [ -1, %entry ], [ %step.i.1, %loop.i.latch ]

loop.j:                                           ; preds = %loop.j, %loop.i.header
  %j = phi i64 [ 0, %loop.i.header ], [ %j.inc, %loop.j ]
  %offset = add nsw i64 %offset.i, %j
  %idx = getelementptr inbounds i8, ptr %a, i64 %offset
  store i8 0, ptr %idx, align 1
  %j.inc = add nsw i64 %j, 1
  %exitcond.j = icmp eq i64 %j.inc, 100
  br i1 %exitcond.j, label %loop.i.latch, label %loop.j

loop.i.latch:                                     ; preds = %loop.j
  %i.inc = add nsw i64 %i, 1
  %offset.i.next = add nsw i64 %offset.i, %step.i.0
  %exitcond.i = icmp eq i64 %i.inc, 100
  br i1 %exitcond.i, label %exit, label %loop.i.header

exit:                                             ; preds = %loop.i.latch

}

define void @single_loop_nsw(ptr %a, i64 %n) {
entry:
  %guard = icmp sgt i64 %n, 0

loop:                                             ; preds = %loop, %entry

loop.header:                                      ; preds = %loop.latch, %entry

comments.author: sjoerdmeijer
comments.body:
I would like someone to write an RFC about this topic, because there has been discussions and questions whether this abstraction is necessary at all. This discussion was spread out over many issues and merge requests and is for anyone incredibly difficult to follow.

Therefore, for the benefit of everyone, I think it will be hugely beneficial if we write up the following in one place: a problem definition with motivating examples, the two alternatives, and a discussion which alternatives seems the best fit for us. Ideally, I would also like to see references to literature. 

I haven't been working on one of these alternatives - that's @kasuga-fj on monotonicity and @amehsan on the proof, so I consider myself neutral in this, but suppose for a moment we don't need the concept of monotonicity, then adding it would clutter the code base and we end up with a non-optimal solution. So, my question essentially is if which solution is the best and why, and if we can have a write up about this in one place that exposes this to more than the 4 people on this ticket. 

For all the above reasons, I think the request for an RFC is a reasonable one. 

I have been asking for an RFC a couple of times already. My assumption was that the folks working on the alternatives would be in the best position to do this. But I can do this too, and can commit doing this next week. I can write an RFC and will do this if someone else doesn't get there first. I will certainly need a couple of days for this: I will go through all the discussions again, collect all information, and summarises it. I will then also consult literature on dependence analysis to look for the concept of monotonicity, and the proof. And in addition, I will consult other DA implementations, for example GCC.

Please let me know what you think, I am of course open for suggestions here.

I am also tagging @nikic here as a heads up, because this is about 2 deeply technical solutions/alternatives, but also about community dynamics how we get to the best solution.

comments.author: kasuga-fj
comments.body:
@sjoerdmeijer For now, I have written down my current understanding. Please let me know if you have any questions or suggestions.

---

# Problem

I will start with an example that illustrates the problem.

```llvm
; Example 1
;
; for (i = 0; i < (1ULL << 60); i++) {
;   A[i] = 0;
;
;   offset = 32*i + (1ULL << 62);
;
;   // This condition is to avoid violating the limitation of the size of an allocated object
;   if (offset < (1ULL << 63))
;     A[offset] = 1;
; }
;
define void @f(ptr %A) {
entry:
  %tc = shl i64 1, 60
  %const.1 = shl i64 1, 62
  br label %loop.header

loop.header:
  %i = phi i64 [ 0, %entry ], [ %i.next, %loop.latch ]
  %idx.0 = getelementptr inbounds i8, ptr %A, i64 %i
  store i8 0, ptr %idx.0
  %offset.tmp = mul i64 %i, 32
  %offset = add i64 %offset.tmp, %const.1
  %if.cond = icmp sge i64 %offset, 0
  br i1 %if.cond, label %if.then, label %loop.latch

if.then:
  %idx.1 = getelementptr inbounds i8, ptr %A, i64 %offset
  store i8 1, ptr %idx.1
  br label %loop.latch

loop.latch:
  %i.next = add nuw nsw i64 %i, 1
  %exit.cond = icmp eq i64 %i.next, %tc
  br i1 %exit.cond, label %exit, label %loop.header

exit:
  ret void
}
```

Currently DA reports that there is no dependency between the two stores `A[i] = 0` and `A[offset] = 1` ([godbolt](https://godbolt.org/z/bGbe8ba6s)), which is incorrect. Actually, they can access the same memory location. Here are some examples:

|| `i = 0` | `i = 2^59 - 2^57` | `i = 2^59 - 2^57 + 2^55 - 1` | `i = 2^60 - 32` |
|-|-|-|-|-|
| `A[i]`| `A[0]`||| `A[2^60 - 32]`|
| `A[32*i + 2^62]` || `A[0]`| `A[2^60 - 32]`||

The root cause of the problem is that DA doesn't take addrec overflows into account (in other words, it assumes *monotonicity* for addrecs without any checks). As a result, DA considers that the values at the first and last iteration of an addrec correspond to the its minimum or maximum value, respectively, which leads to missing some possible dependencies.

# Challenges (?)

If it were non-nested loops, the problem would be easier: just checking the nowrap property of addrecs would be sufficient. One might think that recursively checking the nowrap property of addrecs would work fine, but it may not sufficient. For example, consider the following code:

```llvm
; Example 2
;
; for (i = 0; i < INT64_MAX; i++)
;   if (i < 1000)
;     for (j = 0; j < INT64_MAX - 1000; j++)
;       A[i + j] = 0;
;
define void @g(ptr %A) {
entry:
  br label %loop.i.header

loop.i.header:
  %i = phi i64 [ 0, %entry ], [ %i.next, %loop.i.latch ]
  br label %loop.j.pr

loop.j.pr:
  %guard = icmp slt i64 %i, 1000
  br i1 %guard, label %loop.j, label %loop.i.latch

loop.j:
  %j = phi i64 [ 0, %loop.j.pr ], [ %j.next, %loop.j ]
  %offset = add nuw nsw i64 %i, %j
  %gep = getelementptr inbounds i8, ptr %A, i64 %offset
  store i8 0, ptr %gep
  %j.next = add i64 %j, 1
  %ec.j = icmp eq i64 %j.next, 9223372036854774808 ; 2^63 - 1000
  br i1 %ec.j, label %loop.i.latch, label %loop.j

loop.i.latch:
  %i.next = add i64 %i, 1
  %ec.i = icmp eq i64 %i.next, 9223372036854775807 ; 2^63 - 1
  br i1 %ec.i, label %exit, label %loop.i.header

exit:
  ret void
}
```

As I tested ([godbolt](https://godbolt.org/z/jcbK91nTT)), SCEV yielded `{{0,+,1}<nuw><nsw><%loop.i.header>,+,1}<nuw><nsw><%loop.j>` for `%offset`. However, evaluating this addrec at the last iteration of the i-loop can overflow in a signed sense. Also, as a matter of fact, DA includes logic that effectively evaluates addrecs at the last iteration of non-innermost loops (e.g., the Benerjee MIV test).

# Monotonicity

As a solution to the problem, I introduced the concept of *monotonicity* for addrecs in #162280. That said, I don't have much to say about monotonicity here, since the functionalities added in #162280 are quite simple. In short, I added:

- A definition of monotonicity for addrecs.
    - Regarding an addrec as a function $F(i_1, ..., i_n)$ where $i_k$ is the iteration number of the k-th loop, for example, "monotonically increasing w.r.t. k-th loop" means that $F(..., i_k, ...) < F(..., i_k + 1, ...)$ holds for any values of other iteration numbers.
- A simple monotonicity checker, which recursively checks nowrap properties (currently only `nsw`) of addrecs.
    - So, yes, it's not sufficient. But it's a first step.

## Why Monotonicity / Why Not "No Overflow"?

I like the term monotonicity because I feel it's intuitive and clarifies the assumptions on which DA is based. However, it seems that others don't feel the same way.
The choice of the term monotonicity is also motivated by potential future extensions (see https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/issues/168823#issuecomment-3612636989 for more details). That said, it's true that the current implementation is almost (or even completely) equivalent to "no overflow" or "affine".

Still, I don't think "no overflow" is a good term, because it's unclear which arithmetic operation it refers to. For example, in the addrec `{c,+,a}<nsw>`, it means that all additions in `((c + a) + a) + ...` (n times) don't overflow in a signed sense, but `a * n` may overflow.

## What Does This PR (#176367) Do?

It may be slightly off-topic, but I would like to mention the motivation of #176367 here. As shown in Example 2, simple recursive checking of nowrap properties may not be sufficient. However, it's not straightforward to detect loop-guards and handle them properly. In addition, there are several dependence tests that don't use backedge-taken counts of loops. For such tests, it's not necessary to consider loop-guards. Therefore, #176367 introduces a concept of a *monotonicity domain*. I'm not sure whether the naming is appropriate, but it adds two domains:

- Entire: The whole iteration space, i.e., `[0, BTC_1] x ... x [0, BTC_n]` where `BTC_k` is the exact backedge-taken count of the k-th loop.
- Effective: Certain superset of the space where the addrec is actually executed. Any superset is acceptable, but it must be consective.

## What Does Monotonicity Checker Ultimately Do?

At the moment, it simply recursively traverses an addrec and checks for the presence of `nsw` flags. Since there seem to be some misunderstandings, I would like to clarify that for non-nested addrecs (i.e., in the cases of ZIV, SIV, and RDIV), **the monotonicity check for an addrec `AR` is almost equivalent to `AR->hasNoSignedWrap()`**. So, if we follow the YAGNI principle, the concept of monotonicity may be overkill for now.

# An Alternative

This is one of the reasons why #162281 became too long, but there are two distinct topics discussed there:

- The choice of the term monotonicity.
- The necessity of introducing the concept of domains for monotonicity

As for the former, I have explained my intention above. Here, I will therefore focus on the latter. 

## Problem

I will explain the problem using the Strong SIV test as an example. The Strong SIV test attempts to disprove independence between two addrecs `{c0,+,a}<L>` and `{c1,+,a}<L>`. It includes logic to ensure that the "distance" between them is large enough that they cannot overflow. Concretely, it checks whether `BTC * |a| <s |c0 - c1|` holds, where `BTC` is the exact backedge-taken count of the loop `L`. 

Of course, overflow checks are necessary when calculating `c0 - c1` and `BTC * |a|`. In fact, in the Strong SIV test, the overflow checks are performed properly. However, there are several dependence tests that include similar logic but don't check overflows. I will describe two approaches to fix this problem.

## Approach 1

This is proposed by me (@kasuga-fj). The idea is that if we know the addrecs are monotonic over the entire iteration space (i.e., `[0, BTC]` for a single loop), the inequality `BTC * |a| <s |c0 - c1|` can be rewritten into a form that doesn't require additional overflow checks. Specifically, we can rewrite it as `max(c0, a*BTC + c0) <s min(c1, a*BTC + c1) || max(c1, a*BTC + c1) <s min(c0, a*BTC + c0)`.

I'd also like to note that the original formulation `BTC * |a| <s |c0 - c1|` is based on the paper [Practical Dependence Testing](https://softlib.rice.edu/pub/CRPC-TRs/reports/CRPC-TR90103-S.pdf). In that paper, the expression is transformed to make symbolic computation easier. However, the transformation assumes the absence of overflow, which I believe is not LLVM-friendly. Therefore I don't think we have to follow the paper strictly.

## Approach 2

This is proposed by @amehsan. Approach 1 requires the monotonicity over the entire iteration space, but it's unnecessary with this approach. https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/issues/168823#issuecomment-3591966501 gives a proof that the current implementation of the Strong SIV test is correct even without such an assumption.

## Pros / Cons

DISCLAIMER: Probably the following pros and cons are strongly biased by my personal opinion.

- Approach 1
    - Pros
        - Simple and easy to implement.
    - Cons
        - Requires a stronger assumption (monotonicity in the entire iteration space).
            - This is I was actually pointed out in #162281, but as long as we are dealing with ZIV, SIV, and RDIV tests (i.e., the addrecs are not nested), the conditions "they have nowrap flags" and "the exact backedge-taken count is computable" imply this assumption. So in practice, I believe this may not be a big issue.
- Approach 2
    - Pros
        - Doesn't require a stronger assumption, which may allow more optimizations in the future.
            - In #162281, @amehsan mentioned the case where the memory accesses are conditionally executed thus SCEV cannot transfer nowrap flags from IR. @amehsan called this behavior as "conservative" and seemed to expect future improvements. I don't think it's appropriate to call it "conservative" and I believe such improvements would likely require significant engineering efforts.
    - Cons
        - The proof is too complex and is hard to verify its correctness. Also we need to keep the consistency between the implementation and the proof.

comments.author: sjoerdmeijer
comments.body:
Thanks again for the write-up, here are some of my first thoughts.

First, speaking for myself, I quite like the term monotonicity. It's intuitive and easy to understand, and matches the assumptions DA relies on quite well. I have missed previous discussion on this. I suspect the concern is that there are slightly different definitions of monotonicity? Either way, I don't think this is a blocker in any way, it is a minor thing.

Some quick thoughts on the pros/cons. There are quite a few statements that are difficult to quantify, such as "simple", "easy", and "too complex" because implementations are not complete so it's all difficult to judge and probably remains subjective to a point. So, if we ignore these for a moment, my first criteria would be: "do we need another abstraction or model at all to solve the problem?".

Adding another abstraction doesn't come for free. If we ignore the implementation and the Lines of Code required to implement this, and so ignore if that is a lot of code, easy to implement or read, it is extra mental baggage and another abstraction layer that has to be understood and maintained. Therefore, for me, the preference would be to avoid adding new concepts if we can, i.e. the first test for me is "do we need this at all?".

To answer this question, my questions mainly revolve around avoiding adding a new abstraction:
- The proof is for the Strong SIV test. What about the other tests? Can they be salvaged too by adding extra checks/assumptions/constraints? I get the impression that this is the case, but is that right?
- About the additional checks mentioned in that proof, they look minor. It only involves a BTC>=0 check and a NSW flag check. If this is the only thing, the fix looks very minor, is that right?

If I understand this correctly:

> Since there seem to be some misunderstandings, I would like to clarify that for non-nested addrecs (i.e., in the cases of ZIV, SIV, and RDIV), the monotonicity check for an addrec AR is almost equivalent to AR->hasNoSignedWrap(). comments.author: kasuga-fj
comments.body:
First of all, there are roughly two types of overflow issues in DA:

- Overflows of addrecs.
    - i.e., nowrap properties (nuw/nsw) of addrecs
- Overflows of intermediate calculations in dependence tests.
    - These happen when calling SCEV operations `getAddExpr`, `getMulExpr`, etc.

I believe we can address these two issues separately. Monotonicity is an abstraction to address the former, and it's not closely related to the latter. On the other hand, the domain of monotonicity, which this PR will introduce, is a concept that can affect both issues. Because of this, the two issues have often been conflated in the past. It would be helpful if we (myself included, of course) could keep this in mind.

---

> Therefore, for me, the preference would be to avoid adding new concepts if we can, i.e. the first test for me is "do we need this at all?".

> This seems to answer the question where we need monotonicity, i.e. we don't need it for the ZIV, SIV and RDIV tests which is the set of tests that we try to get correct for default enablement of DA.

Yes. If we only want to fix the existing DA bugs (including the MIV tests), **monotonicity is not needed at all**. I also agree that adding such a new concept is not free. However, I'd prefer to introduce it before enabling DA by default due to the following reasons:

- **Clarify the pass behavior**: The assumptions underlying DA have been vague, which makes it difficult to determine whether certain changes to DA are correct. I think this is a good opportunity to provide a formal definition of those assumptions, which I believe will help us maintain DA in the future.
- **Reduce risk of future bugs**: By separating `SCEVMonotonicityChecker` from DA itself, we can now test and verify the monotonicity checker independently. I've added several regression tests for it. These tests  may help prevent incorrect changes from being introduced in the future in the systematic way.

Resolving the existing DA bugs would be the minimum requirement for enabling DA by default. I believe that making DA more robust is also important, and introducing an abstraction layer would help achieve that. In light of this, I think it is reasonable to introduce monotonicity at this stage as well.

Some side notes:
- Current monotonicity checker is somewhat excessive. It's possible to simplify it and reduce LoC.
- My main concern is the possibility of making incorrect changes in an attempt to enable the MIV tests in the future. The motivation for adding an abstraction layer is to reduce that risk. Completely removing those tests would be another option.
- Even if we don't introduce monotonicity, we still need to consider something like "a domain of nuw/nsw" specifically for the MIV tests.

---

The suggestion I mentioned in Approach 1 in my previous reply is not closely related to monotonicity. It is about rewriting certain parts of the dependence tests into mathematically equivalent forms so that we can avoid having to account for most overflows in intermediate calculations (though not all of them). 

> * The proof is for the Strong SIV test. What about the other tests? Can they be salvaged too by adding extra checks/assumptions/constraints? I get the impression that this is the case, but is that right?
> * About the additional checks mentioned in that proof, they look minor. It only involves a BTC>=0 check and a NSW flag check. If this is the only thing, the fix looks very minor, is that right?

I'm strongly disagree with this direction because:

- This proof is tightly coupled with the implementation details. If the proof is written as a comment in the code, we must keep the code and the comment consistent. That is, every time we change the code, we also need to update the proof accordingly and re‑verify it. This is a maintenance burden, or maybe practically impossible. Notably, there are already several places in DA where the implementation is not consistent with the comments.
- Verifying the correctness of the proof itself is also not easy. It is noteworthy that @amehsan did not notice that the condition `0 <=s BTC` is being missed in the current implementation even while writing the proof. This is not meant as criticism, but I do not think human‑written proofs of implementation correctness are particularly reliable.
- The Strong SIV test is a somewhat special case because the necessary overflow checks are already in place. Other dependence tests are not so fortunate. To apply this approach to those tests, we would need to add overflow checks for every intermediate calculation. Adding many overflow checks throughout the codebase seems a bit risky to me.

My proposal in Approach 1 provides a solution that does not require writing such proofs. This is independent of monotonicity. It also doesn't require additional constraints such as `0 <=s BTC`. Since this approach avoids the risks mentioned above, I believe it is a better direction.

comments.author: Meinersbur
comments.body:
In the interest of progressing, I think the emphasis on proofs does not bring us any further. LLVM is not [CompCert](https://compcert.org/), and the only proof system we have is Alive2 which is in code reviews, not code correctness itself (it does not process InstCombine code).

A proof in the style of https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/issues/168823#issuecomment-3591966501 might help oneself do convince of it working, but has shortcomings.

 * It isn't formal or rigorous enough (either in the sense that a mathematician would write a paper, or a proof system such as [Rocq](https://rocq-prover.org/)). E.g. it does not define what a dependency is, what Strong IV is, assumes $a \neq 0$ but does not state so, assumes $x \neq y$, partially overlapping byte ranges do not occur, etc.

  * There are no references to lines/variables/functions of an implementation/algorithm, e.g. in pseudo code, whose correctness is to be shown.

 * Where there are references to the implementation, it is assuming its correctness, e.g. "Then `c-d` does not overflow (As required currently in the implementation of strong SIV)") which is circular reasoning. 

 * According Goff et. al.'s paper, "Strong SIV" describes the case subscripts are `ax+c` and `ay+d`. Solving $ax+c = ay+d$ is just linear algebra, as done in the paper: $y - x = d$ (the dependence distance) $=\frac{c - d}{a}$. There is a dependency if a solution falls into the loops' iteration spaces, which for instance necessarily requires it to be integers, but also to be within $0 \leq x,y \leq BTC$ (in the paper: $d \leq U - L$, assuming loop increments of 1). This could be a definition of dependency. There is only this definition, and nothing to proof. https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/issues/168823#issuecomment-3591966501 even states "This is a violation of what Strong SIV needs to prove to conclude independence of the two subscripts.", i.e. it proving Strong SIV by referring to the definition of Strong IV.

comments.author: kasuga-fj
comments.body:
To move the discussion forward, I'll write down my thoughts.

What I disagree with is adding the constraint `0 <=s BTC` to the Strong SIV test. The reason is that I'm not convinced that adding this constraint actually makes the test sound. My proposal is that, instead of introducing new constraints, rewriting the part of the Strong SIV test that now requires `0 <=s BTC` ([here](https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/blob/44763603657da164df59d4d3d9a45c95ed7174a8/llvm/lib/Analysis/DependenceAnalysis.cpp#L1364-L1386)) to more "natural" forms so that we don't need to deal with such edge cases.

More contexts:
- The current logic is almost directly taken from the paper Practical dependence testing. It uses "trick" to transform the inequality into a form which may simplify the symbolic calculations, under the assumption that no overflow occurs. Because we are using this form as-is, we now need to add overflow checks to ensure soundness. This can be avoided by rewriting the inequality instead of relying on the trick. 
- The condition `0 <=s BTC` is an extreme edge case. Without this condition, DA can yield wrong results even when no overflow occurs. I used to think that overflow checks were sufficient, but after noticing this corner case, I'm no longer sure what the truly necessary conditions are.
- Properly examining all necessary conditions would likely take quite some time, and I don't think it's worth the effort. Rewriting the logic to avoid such edge cases seems far more productive. Notably, it shouldn't affect the analysis capability.
- Similar logic appears in other tests as well (e.g., Weak-Crossing SIV, Symbolic RDIV). If we choose the direction of adding constraints, we would also need to revisit the necessary conditions for those tests, which would require even more time. Also, I *think* this approach is risky, as we may miss some edge cases again.

---

For enabling DA by default, the following changes are the minimum requirements *for me* to give it my stamp of approval.

- Somehow check nsw of addrecs.
    - Monotonicity is an abstraction for this, but this layer is not mandatory. If others want to delay it,  I'm okay with compromising on that point.
    - But if we go ahead in this direction, I'd prefer to disable all MIV tests (not only the Banerjee MIV test) by default.
- Rewriting the inequality in the Strong SIV test to avoid edge cases like `0 <=s BTC`, as mentioned above. Also apply similar revisions to other tests that rely on the similar logic.
    - No abstraction layer such as monotonicity is necessary.
- Disable the Banerjee MIV test by default.
- Address issues listed in https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/issues/161805.
- Resolve FIXMEs in the tests under the `llvm/test/Analysis/DependenceAnalysis` directory.
    - I expect that most of them will be resolved by the changes above.

I *think* this is a good middle ground and the amount of work required is kept to a minimum.
@sjoerdmeijer Please let me know what you think.

comments.author: Meinersbur
comments.body:
> @Meinersbur : I have the same question: where does this leave us, do we need monotonicity? It is genuinely unclear to me. I don't think we have answered the question, or did we?

See https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/issues/168823#issuecomment-3612636989


> @Meinersbur : can you help? Can you explain why we need this patch?

The use of "monotonicity domain" as @kasuga-fj had motiviated is, that some loop code may have checks for overflow checks not occuring, but without taking this user-provided check into account, we would have to assume that the overflow might occur, and therefore bail out. For instance:

```c
for (unsigned i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
  if (i > 0)
    A[i - 1] = ...;
}
```

The access `A[i-1]` would overflow when we assume it is executed on the entire iteration space, but the check `i > 0` prevents its execution in the only instance that would actually overflow. So if we want to support this without having to handle overflow (or non-monotonicity) situations in general, we can intersect the iteration space with the "actually executed" space. If an expression would overflow in the iteration space, but not in the monotinicity domain, we can now analyze the dependencies where we had to bail out. The "actually executed" space can be an overapproximation, in worst case we need to bail out again just as we would need when looking at the entire iteration space.

I do not know how useful this is on signed numbers, as I don't expect a lot of real-world code checking for indices near INT_MIN and INT_MAX, but the concept is sound. It could even be more generalized, using the "actually executed" domain not only for avoiding bail-outs, but also deduce that some dependencies do not exist because a write is never executed. In this case @kasuga-fj sees a need for it, is doing the implementation work, and therefore has the benefit of the doubt starting their way.



> @Meinersbur How do we know with the changes proposed in this set of patches, Strong SIV will be correct?

Your question seems to assume that proofs are a precondition for any change in LLVM. That is not the case. Apart from references to [Alive2 for InstCombine](https://llvm.org/docs/InstCombineContributorGuide.html#proofs), I don't think I have ever seen use of formal proofs in a patch review. Even the introduction of ScalarEvolution just says ["Add a new analysis"](https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/commit/d934c70cf8171382932c6cb7e921011354e87bfc) while referencing some papers. Granted, that commit is old, when LLVM was a research project, but the [review standard](https://llvm.org/docs/CodeReview.html#llvm-code-review-policy-and-practices) has not changed in this regard, and it would be weird to build proven-correct changes on an unproved foundation. It was so far always sufficient that a (single) reviewer convinced themself on the correctness including corner cases. The reviewer can do this by writing a proof for themselves, read a referenced paper for understanding and compare it to the implementation, etc, but it was never required by an author to provide a proof.

Proven-correct compiler is the domain of CompCert, at the cost of development speed and features, and it still has [bugs](https://hal.science/hal-04828038v1/file/Monniaux_GPL_DEBUG_2022.pdf).

When in doubt, you can start an thread on [llvm.discourse.org](https://discourse.llvm.org/c/llvm) asking the community for clarification.

---

@Meinersbur Thanks for the explanations about "monotonicity domain". It seems there is a misunderstanding regarding the motivation, so let me clarify. As you said, this concept might contribute to analysis precision, but my primary motivation is still about correctness.

```llvm
; for (i = 0; i < N; i++)
;   for (j = 0; j < M; j++)
;     A[i + j] = 0;
;
define void @f0(ptr %A, i64 %N, i64 %M) {
entry:
  br label %loop.i.header

loop.i.header:
  %i = phi i64 [ 0, %entry ], [ %i.next, %loop.i.latch ]
  br label %loop.j

loop.j:
  %j = phi i64 [ 0, %loop.i.header ], [ %j.next, %loop.j ]
  %offset = add nuw nsw i64 %i, %j
  %gep = getelementptr inbounds i8, ptr %A, i64 %offset
  store i8 0, ptr %gep
  %j.next = add nuw nsw i64 %j, 1
  %ec.j = icmp eq i64 %j.next, %M
  br i1 %ec.j, label %loop.i.latch, label %loop.j

loop.i.latch:
  %i.next = add nuw nsw i64 %i, 1
  %ec.i = icmp eq i64 %i.next, %M 
  br i1 %ec.i, label %exit, label %loop.i.header

exit:
  ret void
}

; for (i = 0; i < INT64_MAX; i++)
;   if (i < 1000)
;     for (j = 0; j < INT64_MAX - 1000; j++)
;       A[i + j] = 0;
;
define void @f1(ptr %A) {
entry:
  br label %loop.i.header

loop.i.header:
  %i = phi i64 [ 0, %entry ], [ %i.next, %loop.i.latch ]
  br label %loop.j.pr

loop.j.pr:
  %guard = icmp slt i64 %i, 1000
  br i1 %guard, label %loop.j, label %loop.i.latch

loop.j:
  %j = phi i64 [ 0, %loop.j.pr ], [ %j.next, %loop.j ]
  %offset = add nuw nsw i64 %i, %j
  %gep = getelementptr inbounds i8, ptr %A, i64 %offset
  store i8 0, ptr %gep
  %j.next = add i64 %j, 1
  %ec.j = icmp eq i64 %j.next, 9223372036854774808 ; 2^63 - 1000
  br i1 %ec.j, label %loop.i.latch, label %loop.j

loop.i.latch:
  %i.next = add i64 %i, 1
  %ec.i = icmp eq i64 %i.next, 9223372036854775807 ; 2^63 - 1
  br i1 %ec.i, label %exit, label %loop.i.header

exit:
  ret void
}
```

In both cases, the SCEV for `%offset` (i.e., `i + j`) is ` {{0,+,1}<nuw><nsw><%loop.i.header>,+,1}<nuw><nsw><%loop.j>` ([godbolt](https://godbolt.org/z/6rPGxc58x)). However, DA needs to distinguish these two cases. In the former case, evaluating it at `(i, j) = (BTC_i, BTC_j)` is safe in terms of overflow. In the latter case, it is obviously not safe, as it means `INT64_MAX + (INT64_MAX - 1000)`. Thus, DA needs more information than nowrap properties of addrecs.

That said, this probably only affects the Banerjee MIV test, so it seems like something we don't really need to worry about for now.

comments.author: Meinersbur
comments.body:
> I'm not sure where such documentation should live, though. This might be a slightly extreme example, but when it comes to ScalarEvolution, I often find Nikita's blog post is the really helpful resource.

https://llvm.org/docs/LoopTerminology.html


> In the former case, evaluating it at (i, j) = (BTC_i, BTC_j) is safe in terms of overflow. 

`%N` and `%N` can be `INT64_MAX` and `INT64_MAX - 1000` respectively, leading to an overflow in the expression `i + j` at `BTC` (even much earlier). In what sense is it "safe"? Is it because overflowing `i + j` would be undefined behavior? That is the case for the second example as well, even when ignoring the `i < 1000` guard, which as you mentioned have the same SCEVExpr.


> In the latter case, it is obviously not safe, as it means `INT64_MAX + (INT64_MAX - 1000)`. Thus, DA needs more information than nowrap properties of addrecs.

As mentioned in my explanation, DA can just bail-out (return [confused](https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/blob/114f3b530bae5a195234e3bd1c1328b38b39a000/llvm/include/llvm/Analysis/DependenceAnalysis.h#L143-L145)). Bailing out is necessarily correct. 

I am not convinced about the practical relevance of this example -- in the sense that we should work hard on not bailing out in this case: loops with actual bounds close to `INT64_MAX` do not occur often in-the-wild. Unknown loop bounds such as `%N` which could be equal `INT64_MAX` are. To make this a problem we do not need nested loops or conditions:

```c
for (i = 0; i < N; ++i)
  A[2 + i] = 0;
```

with `N = INT64_MAX`, the expression `2 + i` will overflow at the last iteration (same sample already discussed [here](https://discourse.llvm.org/t/rfc-a-new-pass-as-an-alternative-to-dependenceanalysis/88403/28?u=meinersbur)). IMHO the only way out here is adding a runtime check/assumption combined with LoopVersioning, so we only execute the optimized code with values of `N` that we know do not cause overflow.

If `N` is replaced with a constant `INT64_MAX`, I have not concern with bailing out in such cases: That execution would always cause an overflow of the `2 + i` expression. Even when adding a guard `if (i < INT64_MAX-1)` for the overflow to not occur, my original statement applies: I don't think this pattern occurs sufficiently often in-the-wild to be concerned about bailing out.

Integer types that are sufficiently small that they could occur in-the-wild can be widened to register size, as they are already by IndVarSimplify.

comments.author: kasuga-fj
comments.body:
> `%N` and `%N` can be `INT64_MAX` and `INT64_MAX - 1000` respectively, leading to an overflow in the expression `i + j` at `BTC` (even much earlier). In what sense is it "safe"? Is it because overflowing `i + j` would be undefined behavior? That is the case for the second example as well, even when ignoring the `i < 1000` guard, which as you mentioned have the same SCEVExpr.

Yeah, I'm referring to "safe" in the sense that the expression `i + j` will not overflow for all iterations of the loops. If I understand correctly, it's safe to assume that `%N` and `%M` are enough small to satisfy `%N + %M` doesn't overflow, since otherwise the program is UB. This property should hold because it doesn't have any guard like `i < 1000`.

> > In the latter case, it is obviously not safe, as it means `INT64_MAX + (INT64_MAX - 1000)`. Thus, DA needs more information than nowrap properties of addrecs.
> 
> As mentioned in my explanation, DA can just bail-out (return [confused](https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/blob/114f3b530bae5a195234e3bd1c1328b38b39a000/llvm/include/llvm/Analysis/DependenceAnalysis.h#L143-L145)). Bailing out is necessarily correct.
> 
> I am not convinced about the practical relevance of this example -- in the sense that we should work hard on not bailing out in this case: loops with actual bounds close to `INT64_MAX` do not occur often in-the-wild. Unknown loop bounds such as `%N` which could be equal `INT64_MAX` are. To make this a problem we do not need nested loops or conditions:
> 
> ```c
> for (i = 0; i < N; ++i)
>   A[2 + i] = 0;
> ```
> 
> with `N = INT64_MAX`, the expression `2 + i` will overflow at the last iteration (same sample already discussed [here](https://discourse.llvm.org/t/rfc-a-new-pass-as-an-alternative-to-dependenceanalysis/88403/28?u=meinersbur)). IMHO the only way out here is adding a runtime check/assumption combined with LoopVersioning, so we only execute the optimized code with values of `N` that we know do not cause overflow.
> 
> If `N` is replaced with a constant `INT64_MAX`, I have not concern with bailing out in such cases: That execution would always cause an overflow of the `2 + i` expression. Even when adding a guard `if (i < INT64_MAX-1)` for the overflow to not occur, my original statement applies: I don't think this pattern occurs sufficiently often in-the-wild to be concerned about bailing out.

What I've been concerned about all along is the Banerjee MIV test. Roughly speaking, it computes the lower and upper bounds of a nested addrec by recursively evaluating it at 0 or `BTC`. My question is whether this is actually sound. If the addrec is not nested, then the situation should be simple -- just checking `nsw` (or `nuw`) of the addrec is enough. However, as the name "MIV" (Multiple Index Variable) suggests, the Banerjee MIV test is intended for nested addrecs. What I tried to point out with my previous examples is that, in the former case, applying the Banerjee MIV test appears to be sound, whereas in the latter case it is clearly not, even though the information carried by both addrecs is exactly the same. If we determined to bail out in such unclear cases, then this test would almost always return `confused`, which makes it less useful.

I don't think your example is particularly relevant to my concern, and in general I agree with you about loop-versioning. In practice, "we know `N` is small enough that `2 + i` does not overflow" should be more or less equivalent to saying that "the addrec `{2,+,1}` has `nsw`." If we cannot prove no-wrap, we can simply bail out or add runtime checks, as you suggested. If I understand correctly, LoopAccessAnalysis performs similar reasoning.

So, again, my main concern is about the soundness of the Banerjee MIV test for nested addrecs. What `nsw`/`nuw` mean for nested addrecs is not very clear to me. That said, I'm not sure whether it's worth putting effort into this. IMHO this test is too risky to use in practice.

comments.body:
Thanks for the write-up! I'm glad it feels like my poor English barely got across. Just to be clear:

- The main motivation for introducing monotonicity and its domain is to clarify what each dependence test, especially the Banerjee MIV test, assumes. In the case of the Banerjee MIV test, ignoring conditions such as `if (i < 1000)` would lead to incorrect results.
- I don't intend to develop monotonicity and related concepts in order to implement something like "execution domain" in Polly.

Regarding the following example:

```c
for (int64_t i = 0; i < INT64_MAX; i++)
  if (i < 1000)
    for (int64_t j = 0; j < INT64_MAX - 1000; j++)
      A[i + j] = 0;
```

To be honest, I have absolutely no idea what kind of changes would make the Banerjee MIV test sound. However, at this point, I believe it is sufficient to expose the underlying issue: the presence of conditions like `if (i < 1000)` between two loops needs to be taken into account somehow, and the appropriate way to do so is unclear (at least to me). Although this is an interesting topic, I would prefer to leave it for future work.

My preference is still to introduce these concepts *before* enabling DA by default, as I believe this case is non‑trivial and that clarifying the assumptions of dependence tests makes DA more reliable and robust (e.g., can prevent someone overlook such a corner case). On the other hand, it also makes sense to me that this may be over‑engineering for now, so I do not mind postponing it. However, let me say that I am now leaning toward the view that the very existence of the Banerjee test is dangerous.

---

> Independent of adding "effective domain" information, we need to add wrap checks when there is no `nsw` flag, i.e. cannot assume that wrapping behavior is undefined.
> 
> ```c
> for (uint64_t i = 0; i < INT64_MAX; i++)
>   if (i < 2000)
>     for (uint64_t j = 0; j < INT64_MAX - 1000; j++)
>       A[i + j] = 0;
> ```
> 
> this wraps when the integers are interpreted as signed, without nsw flag, with- and without taking the conditional into account. IIUC, @amehsan [is helping to add those](https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/pull/176367#issuecomment-3826432292) assuming the more conservative $0 \leq i \leq BTC$ domain.

I think we don't need to add extra wrap checks when `nsw` is not present. Just bailing out should be fine at the moment.

The condition `0 <=s BTC` is separate from this topic. This is about another corner case, which is quite strange. It concerns the Strong SIV test, so: 

- The addrecs are not nested, i.e., they are like `{c0,+,a}` and `{c1,+,a}`
- These addrecs have `nsw`
- The Strong SIV test already has overflow checks

Even so, the Strong SIV test is still unsound(!). The [test case](https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/blob/adf7dbc2a8d28d303e0c539f4e7a23561ed3cf29/llvm/test/Analysis/DependenceAnalysis/strong-siv-large-btc.ll) I added in #179664 demonstrates that. Adding the condition `0 <=s BTC` is one of the possible solutions, but I think this is a kind of workaround rather than a fundamental solution. I wrote the details in the description of #179665. I believe the approach in #179665 is more principled.
